title: Living in Boulder vlog 3 - 7 - 2018: YouTube disabled my uploads for copyright for a day

enrichment.summary: Kyle vlogs from Boulder on March 7, 2018, covering three main topics: working on his bike race website (re-familiarizing himself with old project files and converting data), a copyright strike from YouTube that temporarily blocked his uploads due to a green screen dancing video he made, and his habit of listening to Wikipedia entries for 20th-century years as a study and motivation tool. He also reflects on his personal workflow philosophy of switching projects to avoid burnout.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Frustrated and scared by the strike; believes the process was unfair since the claimant never contacted him first

stances.topic: Green screen content policy going forward
stances.position: Will no longer use unknown people from green screen channels; will only use famous people or graphic/cartoon elements to avoid future copyright issues
Considered it but dismissed it because it requires payment
Strongly advocates switching projects at transition points rather than burning out; believes it keeps you working at 100% energy

stances.topic: Listening to Wikipedia years of the 20th century
stances.position: Finds it motivating and inspiring for studying history; plans to do more of it

entities: Tying (YouTube subscriber who made a video request)

claims.subject: YouTube
claims.claim: YouTube made it so I couldn't upload videos due to a copyright strike, which pissed me off.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I was scared when YouTube blocked me from uploading my daily vlog due to a copyright strike.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I had to do research and send messages to YouTube and the person who claimed copyright.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The next morning, I was able to upload my video again.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: A couple of months ago, I did a request video of myself dancing with a European woman using a green screen.

claims.subject: Kyle

claims.subject: Kyle

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The copyright strike led to not being able to upload a video, which was way too frustrating.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I hope YouTube's system wasn't automated, because if it was, it would have really pissed me off.

claims.subject: Kyle

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: From now on, I won't use green screen footage of people I don't know on my channel.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I took the video with the copyright issue down.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I will only use green screen footage of famous people or graphics to avoid copyright issues.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I learned from a test that people can rescind permission for their content, making it sketchy to use.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to do my own background music for videos, inspired by my voice.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I hope in another few days I'll feel like I'm making progress on my projects.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: There are three stages to working on my website: brain candy, getting traction, and design.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Most of the time when I jump to another project, it's not because I reached a stopping point but a transition point.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Switching gears between projects keeps me working at 100% and prevents burnout.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The concept of switching gears applies to studying history, where you move between different books to stay fresh.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Listening to years of history is interesting because it covers everything, allowing you to switch gears and stay engaged.

transcript.text: Hello living in Boulder V-Log. It is 4-8 in the morning on March 7th. I think I did one four days ago. I'm not doing them so much so frequently. I'm not doing them every day now because I kind of can't really fear myself progressing because I've got myself into it.

So I'm working on my bike racer level page and I'm still realizing, kind of reacquating myself with what I built like three years ago when I built it. And yeah, I don't remember how much time I put into it, but I put a lot into it. There's a lot of material there, even though it was with my old computer, which was really slow. So I'm working on here just like tying up loose ends because I think I just abandoned it because I got burnt out.

I think this is when I started working on YouTube. I mean, Google Earth. I was building this. This is the last thing I think I was building before I got this new computer. I was building it with my crappy ass computer, slow as hell.

And then my mom, thankfully, came through and got me a new computer. But like right before she got me a new computer, like literally like one week or a couple days before I had burned out on working on this and I started doing Google Earth stuff, creating like tracing Google Earth trips on Google Earth.

And, uh, and so, uh, so when I got this new computer, I just continued with the Google Earth with that instead of working on the website and on the website, my bike racer level stuff with this new computer for the first time. And I'm just tearing through it. So, uh, uh, but I'm still just converting stuff, you know, like converting miles an hour to kilometers an hour right now. And it's, yeah. So, uh,

That'll be, I'm excited to see what, what this looks like when it's, um, yeah, when I'm done and I can put it together. Maybe it'll be able to put together and look good pretty soon. I'm still, I'm still like working on their website, like stuff that's not even done yet. So, um, okay. So that's the first part. Second part, um, YouTube freaking

YouTube, um, made it so I couldn't upload videos, dude. Copyright strike. Pissed me off. Scared the shit out of me. I went to upload my daily vlog and they're like, yeah, you can. I'm like, what? So I had to do like research and shit. I don't want to do that stuff.

But, but anyway, I, I, I sent them the message to the YouTube at YouTube, whatever they're supposed to. And then I even sent a message to the person who supposedly claim, claim copyright. And then the next morning when I tried to upload my video, um, it was able to upload. But, um, so what happened was a couple of months ago, I did a request video from my, um, YouTube subscriber, Tyne.

And wanted me to do a video of myself dancing with a European woman, because that was when I just got my, uh, green screen set up and that'd be a fun way to do a green screen. So I thought, so yeah, it'd be fun to do a green screen. So I fiddled all night long, made all these women dancing around with me in my room.

And so I got a copyright infringement and that's all right now. Um, but I do remember a couple of weeks ago, um, somebody commented and said, Hey, that's my niece. And I was like, what? So I commented back. I go, I got those girls from green screen channels where they're supposed to do that. I mean, that's what the green screen channels are made for.

And that's why they dance in front of green screens. So, and they never commented back. And so the person never commented back when I emailed them and then YouTube never commented back, but they turned it back on. So, um, so now they had it set up. So a copyright that, yeah, that copyright strike led to not being able to upload a video. That's way too fucking much, dude. You gotta, you gotta let me know.

And then you're supposed to contact the person first and they never contacted me. So somewhere along the line, YouTube, I hope it wasn't a, I hope it was a bot and that they need to reprogram it. Cause if it was an actual person being like, fuck Kyle, that would have been really, really pissed me off. So yeah, I like went to a worst case scenario right away. I started up a Vimeo, you know, uploaded my video to Vimeo, but then you have to pay Vimeo versus more. So anyway, so I'm glad it got turned on.

From now on on those green screen channels, no more people who I don't know. If it's a person, I don't know them. They ain't going on my channel cause I took the video off too. Um, no sounds, even if it's from a YouTube, from a green screen channel, I will use green screen people, but only if they're famous people, you know, who wouldn't care. And, um, and yeah, and I will use a green screen graphics as of now because the way that, uh, copyrights, cause I, I learned it in my little test.

They made me take, um, that people can rescind permission, right? So people can make like YouTube, uh, green screen videos and I can, you know, play like arrows and like birds and stuff, you know, like cartoon stuff in my videos and make a show.
And then all of a sudden I have to lose all my videos because somebody like rescinded their permission.
So it's sketchy. You gotta be super careful. But as far as the music, background music, I know there's a lot of music you can use, but I want to do my own music.
That'd be cool. Like with my voice, like Bobby McFerrin.
That's the theme song to my show. Okay. So, um, oh yeah. And so I was listening to, uh, Wikipedia of, uh, years in the 20th century today.

Started today cause I was, you know, you got decades before. And it's amazing because, um, it's really the 20th century is amazing because you get to hear the progress and cause you know, so much progress in the 20th century, you know, you get to like 20, 1920 or whatever.
And they're like, this is the first, you know, transcontinental fight. The first, this, the first, that. And you're like, whoa, okay. It was that year, you know?
And then you want to do a little Wikipedia articles of them, you know? So like this could you drove a car 150 miles an hour in 1920. And so cool stuff like that. Um, and it actually motivated me.

And I went like, oh, cool. I remember it's that year, that thing, that year. So it really motivates you to try to perform, to study, to perform in the random biweekly history test for the, uh, the years of the 20th century section.

Oh yeah. So I'm super fired up about that. Couldn't do more of that. Um, okay. So, um, yeah. Okay. So that's enough talking for now. Tomorrow when I go on to this, you know, I'll just do another few days. I hope that in another few days, um, it'll get to the point where I feel like I'm, I'm, I'm, uh, making progress, you know, gaining traction.

It's kind of like when you have stages of the website, it's kind of like there's three stages. There's brain candy, which is just kind of like spinning around, like trying to gather and do work. You don't even know what, where the hell you are.
And then there's gaining traction when you're not spinning around anymore and you're actually, you can feel yourself making progress because real things are moving, not just converting or whatever.
And then there's the design where you come to a stopping point and you, it's like getting to the top of a mountain and you're looking around and you go, okay, what am I going to do now?
And that's where you can maybe jump to other projects. Although most of the time when I jumped to another project, it's not because I got to a stopping point.
It's because I got to a transition point, you know, like I was working on Google Earth Rome and I got to the Circus Maximus and then I started making little bikers about around it.
And so then that's how I transferred me into the bike racer level on my website.
It's a great way to work because it keeps you working 100% the whole time and it being exciting.
You never burn out.
You never burn out when you are, when instead of giving up or taking a break, you just switch gears because, and that's the concept of when you're studying history.

I have the, all these books on my website, I'll explain it later, but after in between each book you listen to, you know, the way to decide another book isn't just to try to continue what you're studying because you'll burn out on it.
You need to, uh, go to something different.
So you just listen to the entire collection.
All, you know, like you highlight and then have a review on the website, the entire collection of the history books.
So then you, you, you hear what's interesting and then you, you just switch gears and go to that, whatever you heard, um, you know, in whatever subject you're interested in.
And that's why listening to these years is so interesting because it's the history of everything, you know, and so then you just, you can switch gears and keep it fresh.
Anyway, um, I think that's enough talking.
Oof, almost 10 minutes.
Okay, hopefully I'll talk soon, later.
Gonna have a shower now.
